title: PADLOS: Deformable Linear Objects Segmentation With Pseudo-Depth Guidance and Attribute Constraints
abstract: Deformable Linear Object (DLO) instance segmentation plays a critical role in robotic perception for wire harness assembly, cable routing, and other automation tasks involving flexible structures. Existing RGB-based methods often struggle with occlusions, entanglements, and depth ambiguities. In this paper, we propose PADLOS, a novel framework that integrates pseudo-depth maps and attribute-level structural cues to achieve robust and precise DLO instance segmentation. By leveraging pseudo-depth priors derived from RGB inputs, our model gains implicit spatial understanding to distinguish crossing and overlapping DLOs. Furthermore, we introduce attribute-constrained representations, including centerline topology, local thickness, and physical plausibility, to enhance structural consistency in segmentation. Experimental results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that PADLOS outperforms existing baselines in both segmentation quality and physical plausibility, particularly in complex scenes.

title: DynaMimicGen: A Data Generation Framework for Robot Learning of Dynamic Tasks
abstract: Learning robust manipulation policies typically demands large and diverse datasets, whose collection is time-consuming, labor-intensive, and impractical in dynamic environments. We present DynaMimicGen (D-MG), a scalable data-generation framework that enables policy learning from minimal human supervision while uniquely supporting data synthesis in dynamic task conditions. Starting from only a few human demonstrations – potentially as few as a single example – D-MG segments the demonstrations into sub-tasks and uses Dynamic Movement Primitives (DMPs) to generalize each skill to novel and continually changing scenes. Unlike prior methods that assume static environments or rely on simple interpolation, D-MG synthesizes smooth, physically plausible Cartesian trajectories that remain task-consistent while adapting in real time to variations in object pose, robot configuration, and scene geometry. Our method supports basic, contact-rich, and long-horizon tasks in both static and dynamic settings. DynaMimicGen increases dataset-generation success rates by 3.6% (Stack), 59.5% (Square), 124.7% (HammerCleanup), 232.7% (MugCleanup), and 4.60% (NutAssembly) over MimicGen (which requires ten demonstrations), while remaining robust to perturbations. By injecting dynamic perturbations during data generation, D-MG increases dataset variability, enabling Diffusion Policy and Behavior Cloning agents to achieve strong performance across all tasks and consistently outperform MG-trained policies

title: RMPflow-Magnetic: End-of-Arm Radar-Grounded Reactive Avoidance for Manipulator Motion in Dynamic Environments
abstract: Reactive collision avoidance for robot manipulators in close-proximity dynamic environments requires not only contact avoidance, but also smooth preservation of goal-directed motion. This letter proposes RMPflow-Magnetic and supports an encounter-driven design perspective: in reactive policy-fusion frameworks, the metric activation controlling policy strength should be co-designed with the steering force, jointly derived from the same urgency signal—here, closing-speed from radar Doppler measurements. This unified co-design yields better goal-preserving avoidance than decoupled approaches. Building on this perspective, we develop a velocity-aware magnetic avoidance leaf for RMP/RMPflow where radar-derived distance and closing speed jointly shape both tangential steering and metric activation. We analyze local stability under bounded-residual conditions and validate on a 6-DOF manipulator with end-of-arm radar in static, dynamic, multi-obstacle, and dual-robot scenarios with transparent obstacles. Real-robot experiments demonstrate improved safety margin, reactivity, and goal preservation compared with potential-field-based baselines while maintaining real-time performance.

title: SwarmForage: Emergent Division of Labor in 1000-Robot Swarms
